perturbation.changed_text: 2. Purpose of the Disclosur e of Confidential Information, henceforth known as Sensitive Details. The Discloser and Recipient will enter on discussions regarding future collaboration t o- ward European Funded Project in the field of ΀….] or [will submit a proposal for a collab o- rative project in response to the call (identif y the call) under (identify the EU funded Pr o- gramme) ] or [will enter into or evaluate alternatives for partnership or collaboration with [name of the Recipient ] for the purpose of [ identify the undertaking intended to perform or achieve ].
perturbation.explanation: By introducing 'Sensitive Details' as something that will be used moving forward, the agreement creates an in-text contradiction by only defining Confidential information, but using a different term to reffer to the confidential infromation.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.1 In the context of discussions, preparations or negotiations, the Discloser may disclose Confidential Information to the Recipient . The Recipient agrees to use the Confidential Information solely in connectio n with purposes contemplat ed in this Agreement and not to use it for any other purpose or without the prior written consent of the Discloser .
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces the term 'Sensitive Details' to refer to what was previously only 'Confidential Information'. The contradicted text, section 3.1, refers only to 'Confidential Information'. Because the contract now uses two different terms, and only defines 'Confidential Information', it creates an ambiguity and potential contradiction as to whether the obligations in 3.1 apply to 'Sensitive Details' as well, leading to uncertainty about the Recipient's obligations.

perturbation.original_text: 3.6 All Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement shall be and remain u n- der the property of the Discloser and nothing contained in this Agreement shall be co n- strued as granting or conferring any rights to such Confidential Information on the Recip- ient. Principally, nothing in this Agreement shall be deemed to grant to the Recipient a licence expressly or by implication under any patent, copyright or other intellectual property r ight. The Recipient hereby acknowledges and confirms that all the existing and future intellectual property rights related to the Confidential Inf ormation are exclusive titles of the Discloser . For the sake of clarity based in good faith, the Recipient will not apply for or obtain any intellectual proper ty protection in respect of the Confidential
perturbation.changed_text: 3.6 All Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement, also considered Company Assets, shall be and remain u n- der the property of the Discloser and nothing contained in this Agreement shall be co n- strued as granting or conferring any rights to such Confidential Information on the Recip- ient. Principally, nothing in this Agreement shall be deemed to grant to the Recipient a licence expressly or by implication under any patent, copyright or other intellectual property r ight. The Recipient hereby acknowledges and confirms that all the existing and future intellectual property rights related to the Confidential Inf ormation are exclusive titles of the Discloser . For the sake of clarity based in good faith, the Recipient will not apply for or obtain any intellectual proper ty protection in respect of the Confidential
perturbation.explanation: The agreement creates confusion by introducing the term 'Company Assets'. It is unclear whether 'Company Assets' include *all* Confidential Information, or only a subset. The in-text contradiction here is that the Confidential Information and Company assets should be treated the same way.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.5 The Recipient shall treat all Confidential Information with the same de gree of care as it accords to its own Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces the term 'Company Assets' as potentially synonymous with 'Confidential Information' in Section 3.6. This contradicts Section 3.5, which requires the Recipient to treat 'Confidential Information' with the same degree of care as it treats its own 'Confidential Information'. If 'Company Assets' and 'Confidential Information' are distinct, as the original text implied, the recipient may treat their own 'Confidential Information' differently than 'Company Assets'. This creates a contradiction because the degree of care applied should be the same, but the introduction of 'Company Assets' creates ambiguity.

perturbation.original_text: 3.7 The Recipient shall pr omptly return or destroy all copies (in whatever form repr o- duced or stored) , including all notes and derivatives of the Confidential Information di s- closed under this Agreement, upon the earlier of (i) the completion or termination of the dealings contempla ted in this Agreement; (ii) or the termination of this Agreement; (iii) or at the time the Discloser may r equest it to the Recipient .
perturbation.changed_text: 3.7 The Recipient shall pr omptly return or destroy all copies (in whatever form repr o- duced or stored) , including all notes and derivatives of the Confidential Materials di s- closed under this Agreement, upon the earlier of (i) the completion or termination of the dealings contempla ted in this Agreement; (ii) or the termination of this Agreement; (iii) or at the time the Discloser may r equest it to the Recipient .
perturbation.explanation: By using 'Confidential Materials' instead of 'Confidential Information', the agreement creates an in-text contradiction regarding the return obligation. The term 'Confidential Information' is the one defined. The agreement never defines 'Confidential Materials'. This impacts section 1.1, regarding the definition of the agreement
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1.1 For the purposes of this Agreement, Confidential Information means any data or proprietary information of the Discloser that is not generally known to the public or has not yet been revealed, whether in tangible or intangible form, whenever and how ever disclosed, including, but not limited to: (i) any scientific or technical information, invention, design, process, procedure, formula, improvement, technology or method; (ii) any concepts, samples, reports, data, know -how, works -in-progress, designs, draw ings, photographs, development tools, specifications, software pro- grams , source code, object code, flow charts, and databases; (iii) any marketing strategies, plans, financial information, or projections, oper a- tions, sales estimates, business plans and performan ce results relating to the The European IPR Helpdesk | Page 4 of 8 Discloser’s past, present or future business activities, or those of its affiliates, subsidiaries and affiliated companies; (iv) trade secrets; plans for products or services, and customer or supplier lists; (v) any other information t hat should reasonably be recognized as Confidential Information by the Discloser .
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Confidential Information' to 'Confidential Materials' introduces a contradiction because Section 1.1 defines 'Confidential Information' but not 'Confidential Materials.' This creates ambiguity and uncertainty regarding the return obligation outlined in section 3.7, as it is unclear whether the recipient is obligated to return or destroy materials that are not defined as 'Confidential Information'. This aligns with the definition of Misaligned Terminology, creating an in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 3.12 The Confidential Information subject to this Agreement is made available "as such" and no warranties of any kind are granted or implied with respect to the qua lity of such information including but not limited to, its applicability for any purpose, non - infringement of third party rights, accuracy, completeness or correctness. Further, the Discloser shall not have any liability to the Recipient resulting from an y use of the Conf i- dential Information .
perturbation.changed_text: 3.12 The Proprietary Data subject to this Agreement is made available "as such" and no warranties of any kind are granted or implied with respect to the qua lity of such information including but not limited to, its applicability for any purpose, non - infringement of third party rights, accuracy, completeness or correctness. Further, the Discloser shall not have any liability to the Recipient resulting from an y use of the Conf i- dential Information .
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'Confidential Information' with 'Proprietary Data' in the first sentence, while keeping 'Confidential Information' in the second sentence, creates confusion. It implies that the disclaimer of warranties applies only to 'Proprietary Data', but the liability clause refers to 'Confidential Information', creating an in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.12 The Proprietary Data subject to this Agreement is made available "as such" and no warranties of any kind are granted or implied with respect to the qua lity of such information including but not limited to, its applicability for any purpose, non - infringement of third party rights, accuracy, completeness or correctness. Further, the Discloser shall not have any liability to the Recipient resulting from an y use of the Conf i- dential Information .
perturbation.justification: The original text refers to 'Confidential Information' in both sentences, maintaining consistency. The changed text replaces 'Confidential Information' with 'Proprietary Data' in the first sentence but keeps 'Confidential Information' in the second. This creates a contradiction because the warranty disclaimer now applies to 'Proprietary Data', but the liability clause refers to 'Confidential Information'. This misalignment of terminology causes an in-text contradiction, as described in the 'Misaligned Terminology' definition.

perturbation.original_text: 4.1.1 This Agreeme nt shall remain in effect for a term of [number of months or years ] term. Notwithstanding th e foregoing, the Recipient’s duty to hold in confidence Conf i- dential Information that was disclosed during the term shal l remain in effect indefinitely, save otherwise agreed.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.1.1 This Agreeme nt shall remain in effect for a term of [number of months or years ] term. Notwithstanding th e foregoing, the Recipient’s duty to hold in confidence Trade Secrets that was disclosed during the term shal l remain in effect indefinitely, save otherwise agreed.
perturbation.explanation: The agreement creates confusion by using 'Trade Secrets' which is one of the bulletpoints in the definition of Confidential Information, but using it independently as if it where its own concept. This contradicts the original purpose of NDA since is talking about different concepts in different sections.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1.1 For the purposes of this Agreement, Confidential Information means any data or proprietary information of the Discloser that is not generally known to the public or has not yet been revealed, whether in tangible or intangible form, whenever and how ever disclosed, including, but not limited to: (i) any scientific or technical information, invention, design, process, procedure, formula, improvement, technology or method; (ii) any concepts, samples, reports, data, know -how, works -in-progress, designs, draw ings, photographs, development tools, specifications, software pro- grams , source code, object code, flow charts, and databases; (iii) any marketing strategies, plans, financial information, or projections, oper a- tions, sales estimates, business plans and performan ce results relating to the The European IPR Helpdesk | Page 4 of 8 Discloser’s past, present or future business activities, or those of its affiliates, subsidiaries and affiliated companies; (iv) trade secrets; plans for products or services, and customer or supplier lists; (v) any other information t hat should reasonably be recognized as Confidential Information by the Discloser .
perturbation.justification: The original text in 4.1.1 refers to 'Confidential Information', which is a defined term in section 1.1. The changed text in 4.1.1 replaces 'Confidential Information' with 'Trade Secrets'. However, 'trade secrets' is explicitly listed as one of the components *within* the definition of 'Confidential Information' in section 1.1. This creates a contradiction because it treats 'Trade Secrets' as a separate and independent concept, despite section 1.1 defining it as a subset of Confidential Information. Therefore, the change introduces a misaligned terminology that leads to an in-text contradiction as per the 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction' definition provided.
